items.evidence: The transcript context ("she was able to get additional nag, John Patisse, from how it actually goes up. And Reginald Robinson is in Cambridge, Maryland") describes a Howard NAG member sent to Cambridge alongside Reggie Robinson during the 1963-64 Cambridge movement. Phil Hutchings's own LoC/CRMVet oral-history account states: "I remember getting tear-gassed in Cambridge Maryland... George Wallace came to town... A guy named Reggie Robinson who was at SNCC and he is now in D.C., and I mean we were right together and we fell together and we staggered out of the crowd and back to safety that night." Hutchings was a confirmed Howard NAG member. John F. Hatchett (Pass 1 candidate) was a CORE/Bennett College figure in Greensboro, not a Howard NAG member, and has no documented Cambridge presence. "Phil Hutchings" is a plausible Whisper mishearing of "John Patisse" (sibilants + consonant clusters degrade similarly).

The transcript context ("Holly Fry was also a producer, one of the documentary zone, Mississippi at Summer. He and Connie Field and a couple of others did the movie... Freedom on my mind.") identifies a male co-producer of the 1994 documentary "Freedom on My Mind" alongside Connie Field. Hardy T. Frye (1939-2021) is the verified associate producer of that documentary; he was also a SNCC field secretary in Mississippi and Alabama 1964-1967 and later a UC Santa Cruz Professor Emeritus of Sociology. The Whisper rendering "Holly Fry" is a clear phonetic mishearing of "Hardy Frye" — Sellers's South Carolina accent compresses "Hardy" toward "Holly," and "Frye" gets simplified to "Fry." Sellers's use of "he" confirms male, ruling out a feminized reading.

Same Cambridge-Maryland passage as 22.21. Pass 2's "John Patison" is a phonetic guess with no archival corroboration — no SNCC, NAG, or CRMVet source documents anyone of that name in Cambridge in 1963-64. Phil Hutchings's own oral history places him in Cambridge with Reggie Robinson during the George Wallace tear-gas incident, matching Sellers's narrative precisely ("additional nag... And Reginald Robinson is in Cambridge"). Hutchings later succeeded H. Rap Brown as SNCC national chairman in 1968, making him a major figure whose Cambridge presence is well-documented.

items.original_candidate: Joanne Grant or Connie Field's collaborator (likely Holly Fry)
items.evidence: Same "Freedom on My Mind" co-producer passage as 22.32. Joanne Grant was a separate civil-rights filmmaker (Fundi: The Story of Ella Baker, 1981) but was NOT a credited co-producer of "Freedom on My Mind" (1994). Hardy Frye is the verified associate producer of that film per CRMVet, IMDb, UCSC obituary, and Berkeley faculty page. He was also a SNCC veteran from the same Freedom Summer cohort Sellers was discussing, which is why his name would naturally appear in a Sellers interview. Sellers's "he" pronoun confirms male attribution, ruling out Joanne Grant.
